posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:
Bonneau du Martray is widely regarded Burgundy producer making only 2 wines, both from Grand Cru vineyards. They own 11ha on the Hill of Corton, including 9.5ha parcel of Chardonnay being the largest single holding in the GC. The rest is planted for PN. Among the 1st adopters of biodynamic viticulture. Only 25K btls in the market per year.

Historic estate which had been family owned for 200 years was sold in 2017 to American billionaire Stan Kroenke. He made his fortune on sports & entertainment: owns English Arsenal, NBA Denver Nuggets.

He’s also single owner of legendary Screaming Eagle.

Domaine started own bottling in 1970. But its glory came after Jean-Charles le Bault took over from his father in 1994.

Stunning Corton-Charlemagne vertical 2016-1994 tasting together with Domaine manager Thibault Jacquet.

Winemaking: fully destemmed, all plots vinified separately, gravitation cellar, 12mo aging in 30% new oak followed by 6mo in steel tanks to regain freshness after micro oxidation. Avoid batonnage. Use oak as the spice. Bottle w/ natural corks only.

My tasting notes with (rating N/100):

2016 (94) Herbs, flowers, honeysuckle, ripeness is there. Mineral, tense, precise, sharp acidity. Huge potential. Stock up! More reserved vs generous, ripe & buttery 2015 (93+) w/ oak vanilla spice.

2009 (95+) Lemon, pharma herbs, peppermint. Still a baby. Bitterness on palate. Classic vintage. Pure. Clean. Bright, luminous, liquid sunshine.

2005 (94) Riper, flinty. Warm vintage. Big in quality. Reached its peak, will slowly decline. Drink now, like 2006 Champagne.

The following 2 were recorked at the domaine.

1999 (96) Light speaks in this Corton showing some development, creamy texture & richness.

1998 (94) Very fresh, quincy, orchard fruits.

Only 2 wines coming not directly from the cellar:

1997 (94+) Fresh, floral. Honey on the palate. Interesting guy.

1994 (92) Bit odd & reduced. Seems high on sulphur.

Reds come from 3 plots in Western part of the hill (ex-Charlemagne area) w/ clay red soils.

2016 (92) Game, duck, spices, black pepper. Reductive profile. Mineral.

1996 (94) Ripe red fruits, jammy strawberries. Smoke meat, tart, undergrowth. Light weight. Finesse.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: nice. Two problems—high premox rate and marked recent price increase. Used to be a favorite of mine and a great value

posts.name: Greg Mitrakas
posts.cooked: +1 with Alan’s comments. I have had premoxed 96, 99, 00 and 01 and stopped buying then. Stas, thank you for posting those notes and very glad they showed well overall for you. Sounds like a great tasting.

posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:
+1 with Alan’s comments. I have had premoxed 96, 99, 00 and 01 and stopped buying then. Stas, thank you for posting those notes and very glad they showed well overall for you. Sounds like a great tasting.

We had a very interesting discussion around premox issue.
Actually, last year they assessed most of the remaining bottles in their cellar from the risky late 90s. They recorked all those which didn’t show any premox using Eternam system. They were expecting big portion of premox, but statistics showed only 5% turned out to be with problem. So they released many recorked bottles in the market which should be a safe buy. 2 such bottles were in our tasting and they were in perfect condition.

So those bottles which were released earlier before such assessment might have all the risks.

Speaking what was the major risk factor for premox, their view is that before 90s winemakers were picking almost all grapes aiming at higher volumes and lots of fruit was green and acidic which actually shows in many tastings of vintages from that period. But later in 90s producers turned to picking at a much riper stage. But the wine making techniques were not yet adjusted to it and many used excessive batonnage thus pushing more oxygen into the wines. Those riper fruits lacking enough acidity just got over oxidised and got premoxed. That was the POV of Thibault Jacquet.

posts.name: Kelly Flynn
posts.cooked:
Great tasting! And very interesting premox discussion. Thanks, Stas.

IMHO this estate offers terrific perennial relative GC value. I have enjoyed vintages from 1990 forward, and FWIW have been fortunate never to have had a premox issue.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
+1 with Alan’s comments. I have had premoxed 96, 99, 00 and 01 and stopped buying then. Stas, thank you for posting those notes and very glad they showed well overall for you. Sounds like a great tasting.

We had a very interesting discussion around premox issue.
Actually, last year they assessed most of the remaining bottles in their cellar from the risky late 90s. They recorked all those which didn’t show any premox using Eternam system. They were expecting big portion of premox, but statistics showed only 5% turned out to be with problem. So they released many recorked bottles in the market which should be a safe buy. 2 such bottles were in our tasting and they were in perfect condition.

So those bottles which were released earlier before such assessment might have all the risks.

Speaking what was the major risk factor for premox, their view is that before 90s winemakers were picking almost all grapes aiming at higher volumes and lots of fruit was green and acidic which actually shows in many tastings of vintages from that period. But later in 90s producers turned to picking at a much riper stage. But the wine making techniques were not yet adjusted to it and many used excessive batonnage thus pushing more oxygen into the wines. Those riper fruits lacking enough acidity just got over oxidised and got premoxed. That was the POV of Thibault Jacquet.

I’m no vigneron but one can do battonage without introducing oxygen—roll the barrel. Barrels are usually opened many many times during elevage—for tasting and topping off. Also I worry about the oxygen introduced during tasting and recorking. But thanks for the info.

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked: Great tasting . But what are pharma herbs ?

posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:
Great tasting . But what are pharma herbs ?

Hahaha, sorry about that. That is my poor English. I was trying to say ‘medicinal herbs’, those herbs with aromatics you would find in pharmacy, like herbal preparation / herbal tea. Does it help?

posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:
+1 with Alan’s comments. I have had premoxed 96, 99, 00 and 01 and stopped buying then. Stas, thank you for posting those notes and very glad they showed well overall for you. Sounds like a great tasting.

We had a very interesting discussion around premox issue.
Actually, last year they assessed most of the remaining bottles in their cellar from the risky late 90s. They recorked all those which didn’t show any premox using Eternam system. They were expecting big portion of premox, but statistics showed only 5% turned out to be with problem. So they released many recorked bottles in the market which should be a safe buy. 2 such bottles were in our tasting and they were in perfect condition.

So those bottles which were released earlier before such assessment might have all the risks.

Speaking what was the major risk factor for premox, their view is that before 90s winemakers were picking almost all grapes aiming at higher volumes and lots of fruit was green and acidic which actually shows in many tastings of vintages from that period. But later in 90s producers turned to picking at a much riper stage. But the wine making techniques were not yet adjusted to it and many used excessive batonnage thus pushing more oxygen into the wines. Those riper fruits lacking enough acidity just got over oxidised and got premoxed. That was the POV of Thibault Jacquet.

I’m no vigneron but one can do battonage without introducing oxygen—roll the barrel. Barrels are usually opened many many times during elevage—for tasting and topping off. Also I worry about the oxygen introduced during tasting and recorking. But thanks for the info.

Thibault was referring to stirring when doing battonage. But you are right, it could be performed just with rolling the barrel.

One more interesting point to mention. When piping wines to the bottles at the final stage the last portion has more dissolved oxygen. So they decided not to put those wines in the market and keep in the cellar to offer for inhouse tastings. Otherwise they might develop premox.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
+1 with Alan’s comments. I have had premoxed 96, 99, 00 and 01 and stopped buying then. Stas, thank you for posting those notes and very glad they showed well overall for you. Sounds like a great tasting.

We had a very interesting discussion around premox issue.
Actually, last year they assessed most of the remaining bottles in their cellar from the risky late 90s. They recorked all those which didn’t show any premox using Eternam system. They were expecting big portion of premox, but statistics showed only 5% turned out to be with problem. So they released many recorked bottles in the market which should be a safe buy. 2 such bottles were in our tasting and they were in perfect condition.

So those bottles which were released earlier before such assessment might have all the risks.

Speaking what was the major risk factor for premox, their view is that before 90s winemakers were picking almost all grapes aiming at higher volumes and lots of fruit was green and acidic which actually shows in many tastings of vintages from that period. But later in 90s producers turned to picking at a much riper stage. But the wine making techniques were not yet adjusted to it and many used excessive batonnage thus pushing more oxygen into the wines. Those riper fruits lacking enough acidity just got over oxidised and got premoxed. That was the POV of Thibault Jacquet.

I’m no vigneron but one can do battonage without introducing oxygen—roll the barrel. Barrels are usually opened many many times during elevage—for tasting and topping off. Also I worry about the oxygen introduced during tasting and recorking. But thanks for the info.

I think the Eternam system that Stas mentions they used is one whereby everything occurs in a nitrogen atmosphere - including topping up with a bottle of the same wine. This was recently done at Clos des Lambrays for all their library wines and it doesn’t appear to have fatigued the wines, even the very old ones, in the least. I am generally a bit skeptical about reconditioning but this system seems really serious and quite a few estates are using it.

very interesting but how does one extract a sample to assess premox or TCA before topping off and recorking?

very interesting but how does one extract a sample to assess premox or TCA before topping off and recorking?

I haven’t seen it in operation so can’t answer, but I know that the nitrogen blanket is maintained.
